data.title: Regal joins Hong Kong developers, hoteliers to offer free lodging to Tai Po fire’s victims
data.description:
Regal Hotels International will offer free lodging at a dozen of its hotels across Hong Kong to the victims of Wednesday’s Tai Po fire, as it joins other hoteliers and corporate donors in rushing to the aid of the city’s worst blaze in seven decades.
The hotel will make 160 rooms freely available to those affected by the Tai Po fire for two weeks, a spokeswoman said, citing instructions from the hotel’s chairwoman Poman Lo. The rooms will be sourced from Regal’s 12 properties across the city, under the Regal, Regalia and iClub brands, she said.
The hotels, some of which are certified pet-friendly, will also offer complimentary meals, she said.
Regal joined several of Hong Kong’s real estate developers and hoteliers in offering their property, as thousands of city residents were made homeless by a fire that burned through seven apartment blocks at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po. The death toll climbed to 75 on Friday morning, with several hundred residents still missing.
Sun Hung Kai Properties, Hong Kong’s largest property developer by market value, donated HK$20 million (US$2.6 million) towards emergency support and offered 160 rooms in its Imperial hotel chain for free to the victims.
Sino Group and its philanthropic arm Ng Ting Fong Charitable Foundation offered HK$20 million in donations and 160 rooms in three hotels. Sino also postponed the sales launch of 150 flats at its One Park Place in Yau Tong. New World Development also delayed the sales of 63 units at the Austin Bohemian project in Yau Ma Tei.
Sino owns and manages several hotels, including The Fullerton Ocean Park Hotel, the Royal Pacific Hotel, City Garden Hotel, Island Pacific Hotel and the Conrad Hong Kong Hotel in Admiralty, which is a venture with Hilton Hotels.

data.title: Hong Kong blaze’s ‘heat, flames main challenges for rescuers, not ladder limits’
data.description:
Excessive heat and raging flames that engulfed a Tai Po estate in one of Hong Kong’s deadliest fires posed the biggest challenges for firefighters, experts have said, as they dismissed suggestions that height limitations of aerial ladders added to the blaze’s severity.
They were responding to widespread online speculation that the severity of the highest No 5 fire alarm blaze stemmed from limitations in the Fire Services Department’s ladder apparatus and a refusal to ask for help from mainland China, which had equipment that could reach higher floors.
At least 83 people, including a firefighter, were killed after the ferocious blaze ravaged seven of the eight 31-storey blocks of Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po on Wednesday. The fire had yet to be completely put out by Thursday evening.
“The length of the fire ladder was not the determining factor,” William Ip, safety supervisor of OHMS Limited, said.
The fire service installation contractor said the water jet projected from aerial ladder platforms in Hong Kong could typically reach an effective extinguishing height of up to about 30 storeys.
But he said the water could only help cool the temperature but not extinguish the fire completely.
He said the spread of the fire from outside the buildings to the flats, which was rare, also posed challenges for firefighters.

Given the dry weather and the high temperatures emanating from the buildings, it was difficult for fire engines to park very close to the blocks that were ablaze.
According to the Fire Services Department’s website, it is equipped with different platforms including aerial ladders providing working heights of 42 metres and 56 metres.
The Post has learned that while aerial ladders used on the mainland can reach 100 metres, they would still be unable to reach the top floors of skyscrapers.
Anthony Lam Chun-man, a fire safety specialist and an ex-director of the department, said that the height limitation of the aerial ladders was determined by various factors, including whether the road structure could bear the vehicle’s weight.
He said the longer ladder might not necessarily be better, as the length must be assessed against the width of the road.
The longer ladders were heavier, and the road’s bearing capacity was a critical consideration, he said.
He stressed that all firefighters at the scene “must have tried their best”, and used their professional judgment based on the current situation to save lives.
While some residents also questioned why helicopters and water bombs were not deployed, another expert said they could not help in this case.
Gary Au Gar-hoe, another spokesman for the fire division of the Hong Kong Institution of Engineers said that water bombs were not effective in putting out fires in buildings.
“Throwing water bombs – or firefighters spraying water jets – with furniture burning inside, it would be difficult [to control the fire],” he said.




As flames were still raging inside flats by Wednesday evening, he said it would be extremely difficult for firefighters to enter the building to withstand intense temperatures that could go up to 800 to 1,000 degrees Celsius.
He added that firefighters could only shoot water jets at the buildings to lower its temperature, while waiting for the fire to finish burning through flammable objects inside.
Past chairman of the structural division of the Hong Kong Institution of Engineers Chan Sai-cheong said that a thorough inspection was necessary to determine the full extent of the buildings’ damage, but stressed that they had no immediate risk of collapse.
He added that temperatures of around 500 degrees would typically begin to affect a building’s structure and those reaching 1,000 degrees would have a serious structural impact.
“However, given it has burned over an extensive area and for such a long duration, it must have affected the building,” he said, predicting that residents would not be able to return home for a few months.
He said that because the fire spread upwards, the underside of the floor slabs on every storey would have received the most heat, and he anticipated that the damage to these specific parts would be serious.

data.title: Hong Kong fire death toll rises to 94 as rescue work continues into day 3
data.description: Derek Armstrong Chan, deputy director of fire services, says in the early hours of Friday that the number of people who are missing will have to be counted after the search and rescue operation is completed.\nChan says rescuers will break into each unit to ensure no one remains trapped inside and spray water on extinguished flats to prevent fire reigniting before 9am.\n

data.title: A paper-eating invasive pest could wreak havoc on Japan’s cultural treasures
data.description:
The swift spread of a tiny invasive insect that gorges on paper threatens to ravage Japan’s priceless cultural treasures, historic records and delicate scrolls, as calls grow for stronger measures to rein in the scourge.
The 1cm-long (0.3 inch) ghost silverfish, first identified in Sri Lanka in 1910, was discovered in Japan in 2022. The critter has since crawled into institutions in 19 prefectures after reportedly arriving in the East Asian nation in a consignment of documents, art or other paperwork.
While worms that eat paper are common in Japan, the fast-reproducing ghost silverfish is a new threat which needs to be tackled by raising awareness among archivists and curators to avoid a slow response to the menace, according to an expert.
“Paper-eating moths are pests that have existed in Japan since ancient times,” said Atsushi Tsujikawa, chairman of the Japan Society of Archives Institutions. “I have seen them while handling old documents and books, and the facility where I work also takes measures to prevent damage from moths.”
He told This Week in Asia that insects were generally introduced from overseas “in travellers’ personal belongings or in containers, luggage or imported goods, so it’s possible that this calvum also arrived in this way”.
Tsujikawa said a lack of information sharing among preservation facilities, curators and archivists could worsen the damage before the insects were noticed and addressed.
“To prevent this from happening, we believe it is our responsibility as Japanese professionals, archivists and curators, to raise awareness and share information early on,” he said.

Ctenolepisma calvum, better known as the ghost silverfish due to its pale appearance and agility, is also native to Europe and Central America.
The insect has spread rapidly to other parts of the world in part because it reproduces parthenogenetically, with the female laying eggs that develop into young without being fertilised. One female can produce around 7,000 young a year.
A study by the Tokyo National Research Institute for Cultural Properties, published in the academic journal Insects in June 2023, called the bug a “new threat to the conservation of collections that are of cultural and historical importance”.
The paper largely focused on the insects’ ability to breed and proposed some solutions like keeping temperatures below 20 degrees Celsius (68 degrees Fahrenheit) to reduce reproduction. However, the authors acknowledged that many collections were stored at temperatures higher than 20 degrees to best preserve them.
They also advised “deep cleaning” in exhibition and storage rooms to remove sources of nutrients “such as fibrous organic materials and insect remains”.
Another suggestion was to conduct fumigation activities in the summer and autumn months, peak seasons for newborn ghost silverfish. The research institute has distributed free samples of a poison bait.


Tsujikawa said spreading more information about the threat posed by the insects among institutions and sites that hold cultural artefacts, such as temples and shrines, was critical.
“Many temples and shrines that preserve cultural heritage materials have facilities and systems in place to ensure proper preservation environments and respond to insect damage and other issues,” he said.
“However, there are cases where valuable materials are located in places that do not have such systems, and there is a possibility that serious damage could occur.”
Such damage was not only caused by ghost silverfish, Tsujikawa said, but also “a variety of other possibilities, such as everyday deterioration and damage to materials due to the storage environment, or the loss of materials due to large-scale disasters or fires”.

data.title: Hong Kong rugby players eye differing World Cup road maps as club season resumes
data.description:
Domestic rugby will return to Hong Kong next week after an international break of varying fortunes for the city’s men and women, and for both it represents the next step in differing paths to their respective World Cups.
The men have already secured a place at the 2027 edition in Australia, but a 58-12 defeat at the hands of Portugal in their only Test of the autumn almost a fortnight ago was a salutary lesson in how far they still have to go.
Hong Kong’s women, meanwhile, began their journey to the 2029 tournament by beating both Germany and Belgium over consecutive weeks, even with a squad shorn of some of their most experienced players by National Games commitments.
In their absence, 18-year-olds Elsie Hulme and Jasmine Fitzhenry, both products of the city’s sevens programme, were just two of a new breed who grabbed their opportunities with both hands.
Fitzhenry, a second-row, was described as “a really exciting prospect” by head coach Tumua Ioane, while Hulme’s teammates were taken by her “unbelievable work-rate”.

Ioane said he was “incredibly proud” of the team, who beat Germany 12-10 and then came from a try down at half-time to beat Belgium 15-7.
“A lot has been made of how young and inexperienced the group was, but I was pretty confident before we left of what the group could do, so it’s really rewarding to see them put in performances that we’re proud of,” he added.
With the Asia Rugby Championship to come early next year and the second tier of the new WXV global series expected to take place in the autumn of 2026, Hong Kong’s time in Europe was always going to serve as preparation for those challenges.
Ioane said he had always been confident of where the team could get to in their bid to qualify for the Women’s World Cup in four years, and the past several weeks had only “reaffirmed my thoughts in terms of that”.
“Lots of players were unavailable, but to find out what we have underneath that group, and potentially some have pushed past those who were unavailable, was really encouraging,” he said.
As well as the emergence of young talent, Montana Heslop’s arrival at fly-half allowed Jasmine Fung Hoi-ching to return to her favoured position in the centres, where she “loves running through people”.

Fung, who won Asian Games bronze with the sevens side in Hangzhou in 2023, and featured in the squad for the WXV 3 tournament last year, was one of the experienced players who made the trip, having recently recovered from shoulder surgery.
There was a “huge confidence boost in winning”, the 26-year-old said, but she also highlighted the way the squad had adapted to different conditions, including playing in temperatures as low as minus five degrees Celsius (23 degrees Fahrenheit).
“I feel like it was a really good experience to play against bigger teams and opponents that we don’t get to play that often, good mental preparation for playing bigger and faster teams … it’s a good first step to get us going to our ultimate goal in the next four years.”

data.title: Chinese EV makers step on the gas abroad as hedge against weaker mainland sales
data.description:
Chinese electric vehicle (EV) builders are increasingly shipping their cars abroad to spur international sales ahead of an expected downturn in their home market, analysts say, as Beijing phases out incentives such as tax holidays and cash subsidies.
The carmakers, which can enjoy a net margin of 20,000 yuan (US$706) per vehicle by selling their cars outside mainland China, are rolling out more models and pushing into untapped territories to chase high profitability.
Stellantis-backed Leapmotor, one of the fastest-growing Chinese EV assemblers in terms of sales this year, announced this week that two of its models would debut in Brazil and Chile via a broad sales network.
Its C10 and B10 SUVs would be displayed in 36 showrooms across 27 Brazilian cities by the end of this year, while they would be sold in five stores in Chile, the Hangzhou-based company said in a statement.
“In the near future, Leapmotor will expand our footprint in other South American markets including Argentina, Colombia and Ecuador,” it said. “We will have a presence in all the key markets on the continent.”
The company did not disclose its delivery targets in South America.
Nio, headquartered in Shanghai, said earlier this month that hundreds of its fully electric Firefly minicars were headed for Europe as it took on BMW’s Mini and the Mercedes-Benz Smart brand in some of the developed economies on the continent.
The Firefly models, including right-hand-drive variants, would also target markets in North America and Asia, the company said.
IM Motors, an EV unit of Shanghai-based state-owned carmaker SAIC, said on Monday that the first batch of its IM6 SUV, starting at NZ$66,900 (US$37,978), would be delivered to customers in New Zealand next month as the brand stepped up its efforts to explore market potential in Oceania.

The average net per-vehicle ­margin – the gap between the ­selling price and production costs such as raw materials, labour and logistics – stood at about 5,000 yuan among Chinese carmakers, according to Nick Lai, head of auto research in Asia-Pacific at JPMorgan.
But the margin could jump fourfold to 20,000 yuan if the car were exported to overseas markets where it could enjoy higher prices, he told the Post in an interview last month.
Car sales on the mainland were likely to contract in 2026, breaking a six-year streak, if Beijing stopped granting cash subsidies and tax incentives to buyers, JPMorgan forecast in a recent research report.
Mainland car sales could slow by 3 to 5 per cent next year in a more cautious scenario, Lai said.
China’s automotive sector last contracted in 2020, when carmakers handed a total of 19.5 million units to customers, down 6.2 per cent from 2019, data from the China Passenger Car Association showed.
Until the end of this year, Chinese buyers looking to replace existing cars with EVs are eligible for a trade-in subsidy of 20,000 yuan, while those buying petrol-powered cars are entitled to a 15,000 yuan rebate.
The central government is expected to make an announcement on whether the trade-in subsidy will be renewed in January.
In addition, EV buyers are currently exempt from a 10 per cent sales tax. But such purchases are set to incur a 5 per cent tax from January until the regular 10 per cent tax rate returns in 2028.
The mainland’s automotive industry remains mired in overcapacity woes that have led to several rounds of brutal price wars over the past two years.
China’s output of vehicles, which included buses and lorries as well as passenger cars, could hit 33 million units in 2025, compared with an estimated capacity of around 50 million units, Lai said.
In the first 10 months of 2025, China exported 5.62 million vehicles – comprising both passenger cars and commercial vehicles like trucks and buses – up 15.7 per cent year on year, according to data from the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers.

data.title: Trump admin reviewing ‘every green card’ for 19 countries after Washington shooting
data.description:
The Trump administration announced on Thursday that it would review the immigration status of every permanent resident or “green card” holder from Afghanistan and 18 other countries following the attack on National Guard troops in Washington.
US officials have identified the detained suspect in Wednesday’s shooting as an Afghan national who previously worked with American forces in Afghanistan.
The 29-year-old suspect was granted asylum – not permanent residency – in April this year, according to AfghanEvac, a group that helped resettle Afghans in the United States after the 2021 Taliban takeover.
“I have directed a full scale, rigorous reexamination of every Green Card for every alien from every country of concern,” said Joseph Edlow, director of the US Customs and Immigration Services (USCIS), on social media.
When asked to specify to which countries Edlow was referring, a USCIS spokesperson pointed to US President Donald Trump’s June executive order classifying 19 countries as “of identified concern”.

The order banned entry of almost all nationals from 12 of the countries, including Afghanistan.
The 11 other countries facing a travel ban were: Myanmar, Chad, Congo-Brazzaville, Equatorial Guinea, Eritrea, Haiti, Iran, Libya, Somalia, Sudan and Yemen.
Trump also imposed a partial ban on travellers from seven other countries: Burundi, Cuba, Laos, Sierra Leone, Togo, Turkmenistan and Venezuela. Some temporary work visas from those countries are allowed.
The Trump administration on Thursday blamed Biden-era vetting failures for the admission of the suspect in Wednesday’s shooting, Rahmanullah Lakanwal. But the 29-year-old Afghan national was granted asylum this year under Trump, according to a US government file seen by Reuters.
Lakanwal entered the US on September 8, 2021, under Operation Allies Welcome. The resettlement programme was set up by former Democratic President Joe Biden after the US military withdrawal from Afghanistan in August 2021 that led to the rapid collapse of the Afghan government and the country’s takeover by the Taliban.
FBI Director Kash Patel and Jeanine Pirro, the US.Attorney for the District of Columbia, both Trump appointees, said during a press conference on Thursday that the Biden administration had failed to conduct adequate background checks or vetting on Lakanwal before allowing him to enter the US in 2021.
Neither official provided any evidence to support their assertion.


Patel said Lakanwal, who had worked with US government forces during the US war in Afghanistan, was improperly allowed to enter the US because “the prior administration made the decision to allow thousands of people into this country without doing a single piece of background checking or vetting”.
The programme, which allowed more than 70,000 Afghan nationals into the UA, according to a congressional report, was designed with vetting procedures, including by US counterterrorism and intelligence agencies.
But the large-scale and rushed nature of the evacuations led critics to say the background checks were inefficient.
Under the Operation Allies Welcome programme, Afghans evacuated to the US were granted a two-year “parole” that allowed them to live and work legally and then apply for a more permanent status.
The document reviewed by Reuters said Lakanwal applied for asylum in December 2024 and was approved on April 23 of this year, three months after Trump took office. Lakanwal, 29, who lived in Washington state, had no known criminal history, the official said.
The government file on Lakanwal said he had been vetted by the US because of his work with US government partners during the war in Afghanistan, and no potentially disqualifying information had been found.
“This animal would’ve never been here if not for Joe Biden’s dangerous policies which allowed countless unvetted criminals to invade our country and harm the American people,” said Abigail Jackson, a White House spokeswoman.
CIA Director John Ratcliffe said in a statement that Lakanwal had worked with CIA-backed local units in Afghanistan.
“The Biden Administration justified bringing the alleged shooter to the United States in September 2021 due to his prior work with the US Government, including CIA, as a member of a partner force in Kandahar, which ended shortly following the chaotic evacuation,” Ratcliffe said.
“This individual – and so many others – should have never been allowed to come here.”


The shooting of US troops on American soil by an immigrant is likely to reverberate across the American political landscape. Trump has already ordered the deployment of 500 more troops to Washington.
While Lakanwal was in the country legally, the incident plays directly into Trump’s narrative on immigration.
He has made cracking down on both legal and illegal immigration a centrepiece of his presidency, and this case may give him an opening to broaden the debate beyond legality to include closer scrutiny of the vetting of immigrants.
In a video message posted by the White House on Wednesday, Trump called Lakanwal an “animal” and the shootings “an act of terror”.
Trump called for a “re-examination” of all Afghan nationals who entered the US during the Biden administration. All immigration applications by Afghan nationals were suspended by the Trump administration on Wednesday night.
US Department of Homeland Security spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin said in a statement that the Trump administration would review all Biden-era asylum cases, expanding on a review of Biden-era refugees reported by Reuters earlier this week.
A source familiar with the matter said the suspension included applications by Afghans who had worked with the CIA.
The attack has also revived Trump administration criticism of the chaotic US withdrawal from Afghanistan in 2021.
The withdrawal led to a collapse of the Afghan government that was much quicker than expected. The evacuation of thousands of Afghans fuelled concerns by Republicans that potential terrorists could sneak into the US.
Agence France-Presse and Reuters

data.title: How to make 2026 the best year of your life? YouTuber Ali Abdaal says he has the answers
data.description:
Want to know the best way to study? How to find a career that you will genuinely love? How to reset your dopamine levels? How to have a happy relationship? How to get rich?
Ali Abdaal probably has answers for you.
The Hong Kong-based British content creator and entrepreneur, who has nearly 6.5 million subscribers on YouTube and 1.1 million followers on Instagram, will speak at the Hong Kong Jockey Club Amphitheatre on December 8 to explain how to make 2026 “the best year of your life”.
Abdaal began his YouTube channel in 2017, while he was a medical student at the University of Cambridge, in England. He started by sharing exam tips and revision techniques.
Even after he graduated in 2018 and began training as a doctor, he continued to expand his content, providing evidence-based insights into productivity, personal finances and mental health – even his recent journey into marriage and fatherhood.
In a world of flux, Abdaal is sympathetic to young workers who feel lost and are looking to pivot their careers.

He left the medical industry in 2021, when the Covid-19 pandemic led to a loss of full-time employment. At the same time, he realised that his successful online content and businesses interested him much more than medicine.
“The happiest doctors I know are the ones working three days a week, but to work three days a week, I need to have a side income. So that was what started my entrepreneurial journey,” Abdaal tells the Post.
Today, he is the founder of three digital enterprises: Part-Time YouTuber Academy, a course that teaches people how to build and monetise an online platform; VoicePal, an AI-powered writing tool; and LifeOS, a comprehensive course and online template to help beat procrastination. He works with a team of developers to run them.
Currently, he is working on the Lifestyle Business Academy, an online course that aims to provide business mentorship and help people achieve financial freedom.
Attitudes towards work, productivity and fulfilment have changed since previous generations, Abdaal says. His mother was a single parent and UK immigrant, raising two sons on a junior doctor’s salary. Financial security was what she and her generation sought.
“But the problem with a secure job is that it can often feel like a secure prison,” he says.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Ali Abdaal (@aliabdaal)



Drawing on the cultural zeitgeist of quiet quitting, he proposes that “people wouldn’t want to ‘quiet quit’ if they had autonomy, mastery and purpose”.
He says that with economic recession and AI monopoly on the horizon, the social contract of climbing the corporate ladder is now “kind of dead”.
He sees a return to the independent businesses and apprenticeship models that made up the pre-industrial-revolution economy, explaining that the concept of mega corporations like those that exist today is a fairly new one.
“My hot take is that in the world of AI, we’ll be moving back towards small businesses, small teams and just a couple of insanely huge conglomerates. But I think this will be more fulfilling and interesting for people, whether you’re an entrepreneur or you’re part of a small team.”
For Abdaal, developing a range of skills is the one thing that can better guarantee security. “In starting my own businesses, I’ve developed so many skills that I will never be broke again in my life,” he claims.
But productivity is not just about money and business; it is the essence that regulates life. His 2023 book, Feel-Good Productivity, details science-backed tips to make productivity feel like fun rather than a burden.

It is small, innocuous tweaks that can help you achieve big goals, he says. For example, he says as he sits in a cafe, if he wants to feel more energised at that very moment, “I’d probably uncross my legs, sit up straight and breathe a little bit. If I were at my desk, I’d be like, ‘Oh, let me put some music on.’”
The principles of productivity can similarly be applied to relationships. Abdaal and his wife, Izzy Sealey – also a doctor turned content creator and entrepreneur – set up calendar invites for their weekly date nights. Every night, two hours on his calendar are blocked off to spend time with his baby daughter, preventing his team from scheduling anything in that window.
Viewing the personal sphere through the lens of productivity is controversial to some, Abdaal admits. “Some people are like, ‘You send calendar invites to your wife? What kind of fascist are you?’”
He acknowledges that such a system works for specific people, adding that Sealey previously made notes on all 30 dates she went on when she began to date seriously, including him.
As a content creator who offers life and business advice, authenticity is central but often a tricky task in the online world. In his “How to Get Rich” YouTube video from April, commenters admired his transparency in sharing hard-to-swallow pills. Still, one of his more recent videos on fatherhood garnered divided opinions on outsourcing domestic work.


To Abdaal, authenticity is not about saying everything that comes to mind, but putting uncomfortable topics that typically happen behind closed doors to the forefront. These topics include parenting, relationships, burnout and the unabashed desire to build wealth.
He enjoys taking part in these conversations despite some of the pushback, and his sights are now set on his talk at the Hong Kong Jockey Club and his future goals.
In 2026, he aims to continue growing his businesses and creating online content. “I’ve been doing YouTube videos now for eight years, so my primary goal with it is to just continue to enjoy it, rather than any metrics.”
Abdaal also looks forward to spending more time with family and watching his daughter grow up.
“Hopefully she’ll start to speak soon,” he smiles.

data.title: Eastern boss and Tai Po resident Roberto Losada praying for victims of deadly fire
data.description:
The Eastern head coach, Roberto Losada, said he was praying for the victims of the deadly fire in his home district of Tai Po.
Losada’s team had to play an AFC Champions League Two game against Gamba Osaka on Thursday, 24 hours after the blaze began, but he said he expected this weekend’s local league fixtures to be cancelled.
The 49-year-old did not think events in Tai Po, where at least 65 people have lost their lives, had affected Eastern during their 5-0 loss at Mong Kok Stadium, but he said “everybody has in their mind what is going on there”.
“I live in Tai Po, my neighbours are there,” Losada said. “Once we were in the match, we tried to do our best, but what’s happening affects everybody: me, the team and Hong Kong. It’s a tragedy.
“We lost 5-0, but it’s the last thing we can think about. There are people losing their lives. We are praying for all those families who are suffering. It’s hard.”

Losada was accordingly phlegmatic over the twisted ankle that forced Marcos Gondra off after half-time to add to his mounting injury list.
With key players Yu Okubo, Gil Martins, Felipe Sa and Manuel Bleda among those currently unavailable, Losada said he did not anticipate anybody climbing off the treatment table in time for Sunday’s scheduled visit to Kowloon City, but he added that “maybe there will be no league this weekend”.
Eastern have lost all five ACL 2 games, scoring only twice and conceding 21 goals.
Losada said the loss to Gamba had a “different taste” from the recent 7-0 home thumping by Ratchaburi, because “last time we gave up, today the players gave everything”.
“If you compare this season’s ACL2 to last season’s, there are more negatives than positives … but there are still positives,” Losada said, citing the valuable experience gained by young players, such as gifted defender Marco Lorenz, who “has a massive future.”
“Nobody wants to lose 5-0, 7-0; it’s too much,” Losada said. “But if you win, win, win, you don’t learn anything.
“It’s a lesson, even for me. I have to take the positive things. The hard thing is how I keep the players positive psychologically … but I am fine with that.”

data.title: Canada rolls back climate rules in energy deal, looks to Asia to weather Trump tariffs
data.description:
Canada’s Prime Minister Mark Carney signed an agreement with Alberta’s premier on Thursday that rolls back certain climate rules to spur investment in energy production, while encouraging construction of a new oil pipeline to the West Coast.
Under the agreement, the federal government will scrap a planned emissions cap on the oil and gas sector and drop rules on clean electricity, in exchange for a commitment by Canada’s top oil-producing province to strengthen industrial carbon pricing and support a carbon capture-and-storage project.
Carney is counting on the energy sector to help the Canadian economy weather uncertainty from US President Donald Trump’s tariffs, and is seeking to diversify from the US market which currently takes 90 per cent of Canada’s oil exports.
In remarks at an industry event in Calgary, Carney said US tariffs and the resulting uncertainty will wipe US$50 billion from Canada’s economy, the equivalent of US$1,300 for every Canadian, stressing the need to build projects that can spur growth and reduce US reliance.
He has relaxed some environmental restrictions implemented by his predecessor, Justin Trudeau, while reaffirming his commitment to net-zero carbon emissions by 2050.

It was first reported in September that Carney’s government was in discussions with Alberta Premier Danielle Smith on a potential deal to eliminate the emissions cap.
Alberta is also exploring the feasibility of a new crude oil pipeline to British Columbia’s northwest coast to increase exports to Asia, but no private sector company has committed to building a new pipeline.
Pipeline companies and the Alberta government have repeatedly said significant federal legislative changes – including removing a federal cap on oil and gas sector emissions and ending a ban on oil tankers off British Columbia’s northern coast – would be required before a private entity would consider proposing a new pipeline.
The Canadian government will enable a clear and efficient approval process for a new pipeline to be constructed and financed by the private sector, Carney said.
He added that the new pipeline would transport 1 million barrels of low-emission Alberta bitumen a day, with a route that increases access to new Asian markets as a priority. Thursday’s agreement includes a commitment by the federal government to adjust the Oil Tanker Moratorium Act to facilitate oil exports to Asia.
British Columbia Premier David Eby, who opposes a new pipeline through his province, said on Wednesday the legislation should stay in place.
Other pipeline opponents are also speaking out. A coalition of Indigenous groups in British Columbia said this week it would not allow oil tankers on the northwest coast and that the pipeline project will “never happen”.
The Trans Mountain pipeline from Alberta to the British Columbia coast, owned by the Canadian government and currently the only option to ship Canadian oil directly to Asia, tripled its capacity last year with a C$34 billion (US$24.2 billion) expansion.
But it is expected to run out of capacity by the end of the decade while Alberta boosts its oil output.

Environmentalists raised concerns about the agreement’s implications for climate change, while oil producers supported the deal.
“With this agreement, the federal government risks doing significant damage to minimum national standards that will have broader impacts on Canada’s climate change efforts,” the Pembina Institute, a clean energy think tank, said in a statement.
Industry leaders said the partnership between the province and the federal government would boost the energy sector.
“The elimination of the emissions cap, changes to the Competition Act, and the commitment to work together on new market access are all significant steps towards unlocking Canada’s vast natural energy resources,” the Canadian Association of Petroleum Producers said in a statement.
The federal government and Alberta also said they would conclude an agreement on industrial carbon pricing by April 1 next year.
In addition, the two agreed to cooperate on building the Pathways Plus project, expected to be the world’s biggest carbon capture project and designed to capture emissions from Canada’s oil sands.
The federal government will assist Alberta in building and operating nuclear power plants, strengthening its electricity grid to power AI data centres, and building transmission lines to neighbouring provinces.
It will also release a new electricity strategy aiming to double the clean grid across Canada, Carney said.
